items.title: I've been using Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 one after another (that is, an Opus session then a GPT session ...
items.content:
I've been using Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 one after another (that is, an Opus session then a GPT session then Opus again, and so on) on the same projects for a couple of weeks now, and GPT-5.4 (High) has been consistently more successful.

The difference is even more noticeable in debugging tasks, where Claude can spend up to 30 minutes digging without delivering any result, while GPT finishes with the right fix most of the time.

The only problem with GPT is its conversational ability, or rather the absence of it. Getting it to explain the reason for the bug or the applied solution in a human-understandable way is a skill in itself.

items.title: Let Claude mark your commits as "Co-authored by Claude" and make it easy for lawyers representing th...

Be dumb, tell everyone your code is a recombination of code whose license you don't know!

items.title: We’ve created an agents skill that gives all of your agents the power to understand the most complex...
items.content:
We’ve created an agents skill that gives all of your agents the power to understand the most complex PDFs - with dense tables, unlabeled charts, messy handwriting and more.

Our LlamaParse agents skill can be installed in one-line thanks to @vercel’s skills utility. LlamaParse orchestrates VLMs to deliver best-in-class accuracy over 40+ document types. The skills file allows agents to invoke it when needed to translate complex PDFs into agent-readable plaintext markdown.

`npx skills add run-llama/llamaparse-agent-skills --skill llamaparse`

If you prefer something fast, free, and local, you can similarly install liteparse as a skill.

Come check it out: https://t.co/FIMiMIZgxI
Sign up to LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.title: In a remarkably short time, AI moved from something most people associated with sci-fi to something ...
items.content:
In a remarkably short time, AI moved from something most people associated with sci-fi to something that is expanding what individuals can create and build.

What’s changing is the scale at which a single person can operate. With the right human + machine combinations, people can now take on work that once required entire teams.

We’re still early.

I’ve been working on something that explores this shift.

Stay in the loop:
https://t.co/67ATcDspwT

items.title:
Every prompt you send makes Claude Code worse. This repo fixes it.

Each message fills the context w...
items.content:
Every prompt you send makes Claude Code worse. This repo fixes it.

Each message fills the context window. The more you type, the dumber it gets.

This is called context rot. GSD is an open-source repo that kills it.

One npm install. Works across Gemini CLI, Codex, and Copilot too.

Here's the core trick: Every task spawns fresh sub-agents with clean 200k token context. 

Your main session stays at 30-40% capacity while parallel agents handle execution.

The workflow runs in four steps:
1. Describe your idea fully
2. System researches and plans automatically
3. Parallel agents execute in waves
4. Each task gets an atomic git commit

You walk away, come back to working code. Clean history. No degradation.

The planning, research, and verification all happen in disposable agent contexts. 

Nothing accumulates. Nothing rots.

Open-Source.

items.title: "Galton and his fellow eugenicist / protegee Karl Pearson were not directly involved in the developm...
items.content: "Galton and his fellow eugenicist / protegee Karl Pearson were not directly involved in the development of early computational machines. But Galton’s foundational work with multidimensional modeling — a technique he used while measuring the attractiveness of African and European women — shaped Pearson’s thinking as he developed statistical tools like logistic regression, which is one of the fundamental components of modern machine learning."https://www.theverge.com/entertainment/897923/ghost-in-the-machine-valerie-veatch-interview

items.summary_html: Timnit Gebru shares how Galton's multidimensional modeling and Pearson's statistical tools (like logistic regression) form foundational components of modern ML, tracing their origins to racist research measuring attractiveness across races. Links to Verge interview.
items.content_html: "Galton and his fellow eugenicist / protegee Karl Pearson were not directly involved in the development of early computational machines. But Galton’s foundational work with multidimensional modeling — a technique he used while measuring the attractiveness of African and European women — shaped Pearson’s thinking as he developed statistical tools like logistic regression, which is one of the fundamental components of modern machine learning."https://www.theverge.com/entertainment/897923/ghost-in-the-machine-valerie-veatch-interview

items.title: "Galton Pearson helped normalize the idea that people of various races were fundamentally different ...
items.content: "Galton Pearson helped normalize the idea that people of various races were fundamentally different in quantifiable ways. This kind of racist thinking is what led to Galton and his peers believing that human intelligence could be measured, and that human brains function very much like machines. That jump, Veatch says, played a major role in selling the public on the fantastical idea of artificial intelligence."
items.author: @timnitGebru@dair-community.social
items.summary: Timnit Gebru shares quote connecting eugenicist Francis Galton's work to the idea that human intelligence is measurable and brains function like machines, which helped sell the public on AI.
items.reasoning: Thought-provoking historical/critical perspective on AI's intellectual origins from a prominent and credible AI ethics researcher. Good engagement (54 favorites, 42 reblogs). Important for understanding ideological roots of AI assumptions.
items.summary_html: Timnit Gebru shares quote connecting eugenicist Francis Galton's work to the idea that human intelligence is measurable and brains function like machines, which helped sell the public on AI.

This kind of racist thinking is what led to Galton and his peers believing that human intelligence could be measured, and that human brains function very much like machines. items.content:
What does a Secure AI Factory look like in an enterprise run by humans and AI agents?

@Cisco's answer is Secure AI Factory, a full-stack architecture designed to generate tokens at scale while keeping data private and operations manageable. And it's built on a foundation that makes it real: a deep engineering partnership with @nvidia. 

Think integrated layers spanning software, Kubernetes, networking, compute (powered by NVIDIA GPUs and networking), storage, and security, built to take enterprises from pilot to production with confidence.

At @NVIDIAGTC, I unpacked this topic with Kevin Wollenweber, @Cisco SVP, GM of data center and internet infrastructure.

Watch our full conversation on #CXOSpice: https://t.co/czYCalx0yY

items.title: 💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]:...
items.content:
💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]: https://t.co/Z32D8jP7na v/ @PacktDataML

𝓒𝓸𝓷𝓽𝓮𝓷𝓽𝓼:
🔹Common Conventions & API Elements of Scikit-Learn
🔹Pre-Model Workflow and Data Preprocessing
🔹Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
🔹Build Models with Distance Metrics & Nearest Neighbors
🔹Linear Models and Regularization
🔹Advanced Logistic Regression and Extensions
🔹Support Vector Machines and Kernel Methods
🔹Tree-Based Algorithms and Ensemble Methods
🔹Text Processing and Multiclass Classification
🔹Clustering Techniques
🔹Novelty and Outlier Detection
🔹Cross-Validation and Model Evaluation Techniques
🔹Deploying Scikit-Learn Models in Production

My Review (on Amazon):
I love this book, not only for its amazing content, but also for its brilliant adherence to its "Cookbook" label. The table of contents looks repetitive, but that's the magic of it. Find the algorithm, method, or application that you want "to cook up," and the presentation follows a uniform format from chapter to chapter. This is an extraordinarily practical, useful, and reader-friendly style, perfect for the target audience: anyone who needs to use (or is discovering how to use) specific Scikit-learn tools for their Python-based machine learning tasks. Consequently, this is great not only for individual use but also for training workshops and other educational settings. Python code in a cookbook context has never looked so good. IMHO.

...
items.content:
I got rid of the ConvertAPI dependency in ChapterPal by converting Llambada's mini-apps into APIs.

For example, instead of using ConvertAPI to convert a Word document into a PDF for further OCR into Markdown, ChapterPal now calls this mini-app's backend as an API: https://t.co/MbkAvqM0Zt

I'm thinking about exposing all public mini-apps as APIs (currently, there are 382 public mini-apps on the platform), but I'm not sure if this would be seen as an additional complexity by casual mini-app users.

Would you be interested in using these mini-app APIs by paying for Llambada credits? DM me if yes.

Happy Iranian New Year. 

Had several parties of Iranian family to attend (the New Year weekend). 

...
items.content:
Happy Iranian New Year. 

Had several parties of Iranian family to attend (the New Year weekend). 

My wife’s dad used to run the phone company in Tehran back in the Shah’s day.

The war came up tons of times.

Lots of fear, uncertainty, doubt.

But everyone I talked with wants Trump to finish the job.

They know that if the regime stays in power it means more terror for their kids and grandkids. They all have stories about how Iran’s leadership brutally murdered their friends and family.

Silicon Valley and America’s tech community benefited greatly by smart people leaving Iran because of this brutality and immigrating to America. 

But the cost is immense especially in human life and if things escalate, which is probable, I weap for the cost that is to come.

Freedom is not free, they say, and I weap that my sons are going to learn that in the worst way.

Mostly we feel helpless. It is one of those things we can not control.

In between has several discussions about AI that show people are getting more educated about AI and are getting over their fears about that.

The war came up tons of times.

Lots of fear, uncertainty, doubt.

But everyone I talked with wants Trump to finish the job.

They know that if the regime stays in power it means more terror for their kids and grandkids. They all have stories about how Iran’s leadership brutally murdered their friends and family.

Silicon Valley and America’s tech community benefited greatly by smart people leaving Iran because of this brutality and immigrating to America.

But the cost is immense especially in human life and if things escalate, which is probable, I weap for the cost that is to come.

Freedom is not free, they say, and I weap that my sons are going to learn that in the worst way.

“This book provides an opportunity for fun and joy while developing mathematical skills. The ideas for further exploration given in the book offer food for thought to delve into the world of research and fun, in addition to testing computational skills. Aging in Place is a Habitat for Humanity of Greater LA Program, rooted in Habitat’s...
items.content:
Aging in Place 

Aging in Place is a Habitat for Humanity of Greater LA Program, rooted in Habitat’s ecumenical Christian mission as a non profit organization. Since Habitat LA was founded in 1990, the organization has worked to bring people together to build homes, communities, and hope across Greater LA. Through Aging in Place and related housing programs, donations help seniors, homeowners with disabilities and other vulnerable households remain in safer, more accessible homes, while advancing Habitat LA’s broader mission to respond to housing insecurity in LA. Habitat LA’s latest public filing reports $20 million in contributions and grant from LA, roughly $38.7 million in total revenues shown in LA, and more than $70 million in total assets as a non profit organization from LA.
